title: ITP
oneLiner: Autoimmune destruction of platelets causing isolated thrombocytopenia; risk of life-threatening bleeding (especially intracranial) but most patients have minor mucocutaneous bleeding.

sections.ITP (Immune Thrombocytopenia): **One-liner:** Autoimmune destruction of platelets causing isolated thrombocytopenia; risk of life-threatening bleeding (especially intracranial) but most patients have minor mucocutaneous bleeding.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Intracranial haemorrhage (ICH):** sudden severe headache, focal neurology, vomiting, altered consciousness – immediate CT head + platelet transfusion regardless of count.
- **Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP):** fever, microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (schistocytes on film), renal impairment, fluctuating neurology – *do not* give platelets (may worsen thrombosis), start plasma exchange.
- **Acute leukaemia:** pallor, fever, bone pain, blasts on blood film – urgent haematology review, bone marrow aspirate; steroids alone may mask diagnosis.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Asymptomatic (incidental low count on routine bloods)
  - Easy bruising, petechiae, purpura (especially lower limbs)
  - Mucosal bleeding: epistaxis, gum bleeding, menorrhagia
  - Wet purpura (oral blisters) – high risk of ICH
  - Rare: haematuria, GI bleeding, intracranial bleed
- **Key exam findings:**
  - No hepatosplenomegaly (unless secondary ITP from SLE/CLL etc.)
  - No lymphadenopathy (unless associated haematological malignancy)
  - Neurological exam normal unless ICH
  - Blood pressure normal; no signs of DIC (no acral cyanosis, no bleeding from multiple sites)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **TTP / HUS** → schistocytes on film, elevated LDH, renal impairment, fever
- **DIC** → prolonged PT/APTT, elevated D-dimer, bleeding from multiple sites, underlying sepsis/malignancy
- **Leukaemia / myelodysplasia** → blasts/dysplastic cells on film, cytopenias, constitutional symptoms
- **Drug-induced thrombocytopenia** → recent drug exposure (heparin, quinine, co-trimoxazole, methotrexate) – resolves on removal
- **Viral infection** → HIV, HCV, EBV, CMV – check serology
- **Connective tissue disease** (SLE, APS) → positive ANA, antiphospholipid antibodies, other organ involvement
- **Splenomegaly (hypersplenism)** → enlarged spleen on exam/imaging, often pancytopenia
- ****Splitter:**  Blood film showing schistocytes → TTP, not ITP.**
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Full set of observations + NEWS2
  - Examine skin, mucous membranes, fundi (retinal haemorrhage?)
  - Urine dipstick for haematuria
  - Consider CT head if any headache or neurological sign
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC + reticulocyte count (isolated thrombocytopenia, no anaemia unless bleeding)
  - Coagulation screen (PT, APTT, fibrinogen) – normal in ITP
  - Blood film: confirms thrombocytopenia, exclude schistocytes, blasts, dysplastic cells
  - HIV, HCV, EBV, CMV serology
  - ANA, antiphospholipid antibodies (if clinically indicated)
  - Direct antiglobulin test (DAT) – to rule out Evans syndrome (ITP + AIHA)
  - TFTs (autoimmune thyroid disease associated)
- **Imaging:**
  - CT head: if headache, neurological signs, or platelets <10 x10⁹/L with bleeding
  - No routine chest/abdo imaging unless red flags for underlying malignancy
- **Special tests:**
  - Bone marrow aspirate & trephine: reserved for atypical features (age >60, lymphadenopathy, systemic symptoms, no response to first-line steroids) – rarely needed in classic presentation
  - Thrombopoietin level (research; not routine)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - No single validated clinical diagnostic criteria – diagnosis of exclusion
  - Typical features: isolated thrombocytopenia <100 x10⁹/L, normal film (except large platelets), no other cause identified
  - Response to corticosteroids supports diagnosis
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood film** – failure to examine may miss TTP (schistocytes), leukaemia (blasts), or pseudothrombocytopenia (platelet clumps on EDTA)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If major bleeding (including ICH) OR platelets <10 x10⁹/L with high bleeding risk: give **platelet transfusion** (1 adult therapeutic dose) – *not* routine for asymptomatic low counts
  - Start **dexamethasone 40 mg po/IV once daily for 4 days** OR **prednisolone 1 mg/kg po once daily** (UK first-line)
  - If severe bleeding or need for rapid platelet rise (e.g. neurosurgery): **IVIG 1 g/kg IV over 2 days** (or 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days)
  - If life-threatening bleeding: **tranexamic acid 1 g IV** (if no DIC) + platelet transfusion
  - Give **PPI cover** (e.g. omeprazole 20 mg po daily) with high-dose steroids
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**
  - Monitor platelet count daily
  - Assess bleeding – use validated bleeding score (e.g. ITP-BAT)
  - Taper steroids gradually over 2–4 weeks once count >50 x10⁹/L
  - If no response to steroids by day 7–10, discuss with haematology – consider IVIG, rituximab, or TPO receptor agonist
  - Avoid NSAIDs, antiplatelet agents, anticoagulants
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Chronic ITP (>12 months): TPO receptor agonists (eltrombopag, romiplostim) – start after specialist assessment
  - Rituximab (anti-CD20) – steroid-sparing, but delayed response
  - Splenectomy – highly effective, but reserved for refractory cases (need polysaccharide vaccinations 2+ weeks pre-op)
  - Avoid unnecessary splenectomy in children
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: nil specific unless bleeding
  - VTE prophylaxis: withhold if platelets <50 x10⁹/L; if count >50, consider LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg sc od) – balance risk of thrombosis vs bleeding
  - Diet: no restrictions; avoid aspirin/other NSAIDs
  - Observations: daily weight + NEWS2, monitor stool/urine for blood
  - Avoid intramuscular injections if platelets <50
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Prednisolone: 1 mg/kg po once daily (max 100 mg daily) for 2–4 weeks, then taper over 6–12 weeks
  - Dexamethasone: 40 mg po/IV once daily × 4 days (pulse) – used in severe cases or as first-line
  - IVIG: 1 g/kg IV total dose (usually split over 2 consecutive days) OR 0.4 g/kg/day IV for 5 days
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Eltrombopag: 50 mg po once daily (adjust based on platelet count; max 75 mg) – [check local policy]
  - Romiplostim: 1 mcg/kg sc weekly (increase by 1 mcg/kg weekly to achieve platelet >50) – [check local policy]
  - Rituximab: 375 mg/m² IV weekly × 4 weeks
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Steroids: avoid in active infection (unless severe ITP); taper slowly to avoid adrenal insufficiency
  - TPO-RA: do not use if myelodysplasia or leukaemia – may increase blast proliferation
  - IVIG: caution with IgA deficiency, renal impairment, migraine
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Eltrombopag: reduce dose in hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C) – [check local policy]
  - IVIG: use with caution in renal impairment; consider slow infusion rate
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving platelet transfusion for stable low count without bleeding** – risks alloimmunisation and future refractoriness; only indicated for life-threatening bleeding or pre-procedure.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Intracranial haemorrhage (rare, 1–2%; high mortality)
  - GI bleeding, haematuria, epistaxis requiring intervention
  - Steroid-related: hyperglycaemia, psychosis, gastritis, infection risk
  - IVIG: anaphylaxis (in IgA deficiency), aseptic meningitis, renal impairment
- **Chronic:**
  - Fatigue, reduced QoL
  - Secondary immune deficiencies (if on immunosuppressants long-term)
  - Post-splenectomy infections: encapsulated organisms (pneumococcus, meningococcus, H. influenzae) – give vaccinations 2 weeks pre-op
  - Thrombosis (rare) with TPO-RA use
  - Osteonecrosis (prolonged steroid use)
sections.Prognosis:
- 80% of adults achieve platelet >50 x10⁹/L with first-line steroids; ~30% remain chronic (>12 months)
- ICH mortality ~20–30% if occurs
- Splenectomy leads to durable remission in ~70%
- Overall normal life expectancy in most patients
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Always review the blood film yourself** – you can spot schistocytes or blasts before the lab calls.
2. **Start PPI immediately with any steroid dose** – prevents GI bleeding, a common cause of morbidity in ITP.
3. **Vaccinate against encapsulated bacteria 2–4 weeks before splenectomy** – gives best antibody response; delay surgery if not done.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Do not give routine platelet transfusion** for an isolated low count without bleeding – it wastes platelets and may cause alloimmunisation.
2. **Do not assume ITP without checking HIV, HCV, and blood film** – missing viral-associated thrombocytopenia changes management (simple ART or HCV therapy).
3. **Do not use loperamide or NSAIDs** for GI upset – both worsen bleeding risk; use simple antacids or paracetamol instead.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening headache, especially sudden severe (think ICH)
  - Black tarry stools, frank blood in vomit or urine
  - Extensive new petechiae/purpura, especially oral blisters
- **Follow-up:**
  - Haematology clinic within 1–2 weeks for count check and steroid taper plan
  - Repeat FBC weekly until platelet >50 x10⁹/L and stable
  - Full vaccine history review (especially if splenectomy considered)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / haematology reg immediately if:**
  - Platelets <10 x10⁹/L with evidence of active bleeding
  - Suspected ICH (NEWS2 >5, GCS drop, focal neurology) – call crash team if unstable
  - Worsening abdominal pain, hypotension, melaena (GI bleed)
  - Fever + rigors during IVIG infusion (possible anaphylaxis)
- **ICU referral:** if ICH requiring neurosurgical intervention, or massive transfusion requirement, or multi-organ failure from TTP (if misdiagnosed)

rawMarkdown:
## ITP (Immune Thrombocytopenia)
**One-liner:** Autoimmune destruction of platelets causing isolated thrombocytopenia; risk of life-threatening bleeding (especially intracranial) but most patients have minor mucocutaneous bleeding.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Intracranial haemorrhage (ICH):** sudden severe headache, focal neurology, vomiting, altered consciousness – immediate CT head + platelet transfusion regardless of count.
- **Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP):** fever, microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (schistocytes on film), renal impairment, fluctuating neurology – *do not* give platelets (may worsen thrombosis), start plasma exchange.
- **Acute leukaemia:** pallor, fever, bone pain, blasts on blood film – urgent haematology review, bone marrow aspirate; steroids alone may mask diagnosis.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Asymptomatic (incidental low count on routine bloods)
  - Easy bruising, petechiae, purpura (especially lower limbs)
  - Mucosal bleeding: epistaxis, gum bleeding, menorrhagia
  - Wet purpura (oral blisters) – high risk of ICH
  - Rare: haematuria, GI bleeding, intracranial bleed
- **Key exam findings:**
  - No hepatosplenomegaly (unless secondary ITP from SLE/CLL etc.)
  - No lymphadenopathy (unless associated haematological malignancy)
  - Neurological exam normal unless ICH
  - Blood pressure normal; no signs of DIC (no acral cyanosis, no bleeding from multiple sites)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **TTP / HUS** → schistocytes on film, elevated LDH, renal impairment, fever
- **DIC** → prolonged PT/APTT, elevated D-dimer, bleeding from multiple sites, underlying sepsis/malignancy
- **Leukaemia / myelodysplasia** → blasts/dysplastic cells on film, cytopenias, constitutional symptoms
- **Drug-induced thrombocytopenia** → recent drug exposure (heparin, quinine, co-trimoxazole, methotrexate) – resolves on removal
- **Viral infection** → HIV, HCV, EBV, CMV – check serology
- **Connective tissue disease** (SLE, APS) → positive ANA, antiphospholipid antibodies, other organ involvement
- **Splenomegaly (hypersplenism)** → enlarged spleen on exam/imaging, often pancytopenia
- ****Splitter:**  Blood film showing schistocytes → TTP, not ITP.**  

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Full set of observations + NEWS2
  - Examine skin, mucous membranes, fundi (retinal haemorrhage?)
  - Urine dipstick for haematuria
  - Consider CT head if any headache or neurological sign
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC + reticulocyte count (isolated thrombocytopenia, no anaemia unless bleeding)
  - Coagulation screen (PT, APTT, fibrinogen) – normal in ITP
  - Blood film: confirms thrombocytopenia, exclude schistocytes, blasts, dysplastic cells
  - HIV, HCV, EBV, CMV serology
  - ANA, antiphospholipid antibodies (if clinically indicated)
  - Direct antiglobulin test (DAT) – to rule out Evans syndrome (ITP + AIHA)
  - TFTs (autoimmune thyroid disease associated)
- **Imaging:**
  - CT head: if headache, neurological signs, or platelets <10 x10⁹/L with bleeding
  - No routine chest/abdo imaging unless red flags for underlying malignancy
- **Special tests:**
  - Bone marrow aspirate & trephine: reserved for atypical features (age >60, lymphadenopathy, systemic symptoms, no response to first-line steroids) – rarely needed in classic presentation
  - Thrombopoietin level (research; not routine)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - No single validated clinical diagnostic criteria – diagnosis of exclusion
  - Typical features: isolated thrombocytopenia <100 x10⁹/L, normal film (except large platelets), no other cause identified
  - Response to corticosteroids supports diagnosis
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood film** – failure to examine may miss TTP (schistocytes), leukaemia (blasts), or pseudothrombocytopenia (platelet clumps on EDTA)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If major bleeding (including ICH) OR platelets <10 x10⁹/L with high bleeding risk: give **platelet transfusion** (1 adult therapeutic dose) – *not* routine for asymptomatic low counts
  - Start **dexamethasone 40 mg po/IV once daily for 4 days** OR **prednisolone 1 mg/kg po once daily** (UK first-line)
  - If severe bleeding or need for rapid platelet rise (e.g. neurosurgery): **IVIG 1 g/kg IV over 2 days** (or 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days)
  - If life-threatening bleeding: **tranexamic acid 1 g IV** (if no DIC) + platelet transfusion
  - Give **PPI cover** (e.g. omeprazole 20 mg po daily) with high-dose steroids
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**
  - Monitor platelet count daily
  - Assess bleeding – use validated bleeding score (e.g. ITP-BAT)
  - Taper steroids gradually over 2–4 weeks once count >50 x10⁹/L
  - If no response to steroids by day 7–10, discuss with haematology – consider IVIG, rituximab, or TPO receptor agonist
  - Avoid NSAIDs, antiplatelet agents, anticoagulants
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Chronic ITP (>12 months): TPO receptor agonists (eltrombopag, romiplostim) – start after specialist assessment
  - Rituximab (anti-CD20) – steroid-sparing, but delayed response
  - Splenectomy – highly effective, but reserved for refractory cases (need polysaccharide vaccinations 2+ weeks pre-op)
  - Avoid unnecessary splenectomy in children
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: nil specific unless bleeding
  - VTE prophylaxis: withhold if platelets <50 x10⁹/L; if count >50, consider LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg sc od) – balance risk of thrombosis vs bleeding
  - Diet: no restrictions; avoid aspirin/other NSAIDs
  - Observations: daily weight + NEWS2, monitor stool/urine for blood
  - Avoid intramuscular injections if platelets <50

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Prednisolone: 1 mg/kg po once daily (max 100 mg daily) for 2–4 weeks, then taper over 6–12 weeks
  - Dexamethasone: 40 mg po/IV once daily × 4 days (pulse) – used in severe cases or as first-line
  - IVIG: 1 g/kg IV total dose (usually split over 2 consecutive days) OR 0.4 g/kg/day IV for 5 days
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Eltrombopag: 50 mg po once daily (adjust based on platelet count; max 75 mg) – [check local policy]
  - Romiplostim: 1 mcg/kg sc weekly (increase by 1 mcg/kg weekly to achieve platelet >50) – [check local policy]
  - Rituximab: 375 mg/m² IV weekly × 4 weeks
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Steroids: avoid in active infection (unless severe ITP); taper slowly to avoid adrenal insufficiency
  - TPO-RA: do not use if myelodysplasia or leukaemia – may increase blast proliferation
  - IVIG: caution with IgA deficiency, renal impairment, migraine
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Eltrombopag: reduce dose in hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C) – [check local policy]
  - IVIG: use with caution in renal impairment; consider slow infusion rate
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving platelet transfusion for stable low count without bleeding** – risks alloimmunisation and future refractoriness; only indicated for life-threatening bleeding or pre-procedure.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Intracranial haemorrhage (rare, 1–2%; high mortality)
  - GI bleeding, haematuria, epistaxis requiring intervention
  - Steroid-related: hyperglycaemia, psychosis, gastritis, infection risk
  - IVIG: anaphylaxis (in IgA deficiency), aseptic meningitis, renal impairment
- **Chronic:**
  - Fatigue, reduced QoL
  - Secondary immune deficiencies (if on immunosuppressants long-term)
  - Post-splenectomy infections: encapsulated organisms (pneumococcus, meningococcus, H. influenzae) – give vaccinations 2 weeks pre-op
  - Thrombosis (rare) with TPO-RA use
  - Osteonecrosis (prolonged steroid use)

### Prognosis
- 80% of adults achieve platelet >50 x10⁹/L with first-line steroids; ~30% remain chronic (>12 months)
- ICH mortality ~20–30% if occurs
- Splenectomy leads to durable remission in ~70%
- Overall normal life expectancy in most patients

### Pearls (positive actions)
1. **Always review the blood film yourself** – you can spot schistocytes or blasts before the lab calls.
2. **Start PPI immediately with any steroid dose** – prevents GI bleeding, a common cause of morbidity in ITP.
3. **Vaccinate against encapsulated bacteria 2–4 weeks before splenectomy** – gives best antibody response; delay surgery if not done.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
1. **Do not give routine platelet transfusion** for an isolated low count without bleeding – it wastes platelets and may cause alloimmunisation.
2. **Do not assume ITP without checking HIV, HCV, and blood film** – missing viral-associated thrombocytopenia changes management (simple ART or HCV therapy).
3. **Do not use loperamide or NSAIDs** for GI upset – both worsen bleeding risk; use simple antacids or paracetamol instead.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening headache, especially sudden severe (think ICH)
  - Black tarry stools, frank blood in vomit or urine
  - Extensive new petechiae/purpura, especially oral blisters
- **Follow-up:**
  - Haematology clinic within 1–2 weeks for count check and steroid taper plan
  - Repeat FBC weekly until platelet >50 x10⁹/L and stable
  - Full vaccine history review (especially if splenectomy considered)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / haematology reg immediately if:**
  - Platelets <10 x10⁹/L with evidence of active bleeding
  - Suspected ICH (NEWS2 >5, GCS drop, focal neurology) – call crash team if unstable
  - Worsening abdominal pain, hypotension, melaena (GI bleed)
  - Fever + rigors during IVIG infusion (possible anaphylaxis)
- **ICU referral:** if ICH requiring neurosurgical intervention, or massive transfusion requirement, or multi-organ failure from TTP (if misdiagnosed)
